text: # Page 1 Computer Science Electives and Clusters Computer Science electives belong to one or more groupings called clusters. Undergraduate students with the proper prerequisites are permitted and encouraged to take graduate-level Computer Science courses. Students should note, however, that in some cases, a graduate-level course should not be selected if the student has completed a similar undergraduate-level course. Such details may be found in the course catalog description. The CS Undergraduate Handbook also contains a document listing pairs of similar graduate and undergraduate courses. Some course numbers are generic and represent vehicles for faculty to introduce new courses in one or perhaps more than one cluster. At the undergraduate level there are seminar courses and at the graduate level there are topics courses. The entries for these generic course numbers, for example, CSCI 539 or CSCI 759, are identified as generic in the tables below and show the potential cluster(s) that might be associated with individual offerings of these generic course numbers. When specific seminar or topic instances are approved to be offered, we list them here and indicate the actual cluster or clusters they are associated with. Be sure to consult the notes table that follows the listings of undergraduate and graduate Computer Science Electives for important additional information and restrictions. Vertically Integrated Project (VIP) course offerings provide an opportunity for students to engage in ambitious, long-term, large-scale multidisciplinary project teams led by faculty. More information about how participation in a VIP counts toward a Computer Science BS degree is found in the CS Undergraduate Handbook. X Intelligence: Theory and Practice of Gen AI 740 Programming Language X X Theory 742 Compiler Construction X 749 Topics in Programming X X Languages and Tools (generic) 749 Topics in Programming X X Languages and Tools: Verification using Proof Assistants 750 Fundamentals of Cloud X Computing 759 Topics in Systems X X X (generic) 761 Topics in Advanced X Algorithms (generic) 761 Topics in Advanced X Algorithms: Topics in Combinatorial Computing 761 Topics in Advanced X Algorithms: Distributed Algorithms 764 Quantum-Resistant X X Cryptography 769 Topics in Theory (generic) X X # Page 7 Course Graduate Course Name Cluster Number AIN AOS CGM DSC DSS PLT SEC THR CSCI- 769 Topics in Theory: X X Quantum Computing Principles and Applications 787 Masters Thesis Proposal (5) and Preparation 799 Computer Science (4) Graduate Independent Study # Page 8 Notes Containing Additional Information or Restrictions Note Information/Restrictions 1 Undergraduate students who take both CSCI 320 and CSCI 420/420H are not permitted to take CSCI 620 as a Computer Science elective or for credit toward a Computer Science degree. Many graduate-level Data Science courses that require CSCI 620 as a prerequisite also permit students to enroll if they have completed CSCI 320 and CSCI 420/420H. Some graduate-level Data Science courses that require CSCI 620 as a prerequisite will permit students to enroll if they have completed just CSCI 320. 2 CSCI 541 and CSCI 641 are co-listed. Students are permitted to take the courses for credit more than once, provided each instance deals with a different paradigm and language. 3 CSCI 589 Undergraduate Thesis Proposal and Preparation, and CSCI 590 Undergraduate Thesis are opportunities for undergraduate students to pursue an undergraduate thesis. In the first course, the student develops the thesis proposal; in the second course the student produces the thesis. In each case, a student must work with a faculty member who agrees to mentor the student. The proposal form offers the student and faculty member a chance to propose that the thesis fit in no cluster or be associated with one specific cluster – the proposed cluster designation is reviewed by the coordinators when the proposal form is submitted. Approval of the thesis proposal includes approval of the proposed cluster designation, if one is specified. More information on CSCI 589 and CSCI 590 is available in the CS Undergraduate Handbook. 4 Independent study projects are proposed by a student working

the thesis fit in no cluster or be associated with one specific cluster – the proposed cluster designation is reviewed by the coordinators when the proposal form is submitted. Approval of the thesis proposal includes approval of the proposed cluster designation, if one is specified. More information on CSCI 589 and CSCI 590 is available in the CS Undergraduate Handbook. 4 Independent study projects are proposed by a student working in conjunction with a faculty member who agrees to sponsor the project. The proposal form offers the student and faculty member a chance to propose that the project fit in no cluster or be associated with one specific cluster – the proposed cluster designation is reviewed by the coordinators when the proposal form is submitted. Approval of the independent study project includes approval of the proposed cluster designation, if one is specified. More information on CSCI 599 Computer Science Undergraduate Independent Study is available in the CS Undergraduate Handbook. 5 CSCI 787 Masters Thesis Proposal and Preparation is for graduate students who plan to pursue the thesis option, and will apply a course in their program of study devoted to preparation of the thesis proposal. A student must work with a faculty member who agrees to mentor the student. The proposal form offers the student and faculty member a chance to propose that the thesis fit in no cluster or be associated with one specific cluster – the proposed cluster designation is reviewed by the coordinators when the proposal form is submitted. Approval of the thesis proposal includes approval of the proposed cluster designation, if one is specified. More information on CSCI 787 is available in the CS Undergraduate Handbook. # Page 9 Guide to Computer Science Clusters AIN Artificial Intelligence DSS Distributed Systems AOS Architecture and Operating PLT Programming Languages and Systems Tools CGM Computer Graphics and THR Theory Computational Media DSC Data Science SEC Security Partial Guide to RIT Course Numbering Scheme Course Number Range Interpretation 101-199 Introductory lower-division undergraduate courses 200-299 Lower-division undergraduate courses 300-499 Upper-division undergraduate courses 500-599 Advanced upper-division undergraduate courses 600-699 Introductory graduate courses 700-799 Advanced graduate courses

Title: Need advice on CS core classes at RIT Subreddit: rit Source: RIT CS student discussion (curated summary) Post body: I'm finishing my first year in CS at RIT and I want to plan ahead. Can anyone give advice on the core CS classes — which ones are the hardest, which matter most for jobs, and what order makes sense? I'm not sure whether to try to accelerate or just follow the default flowchart. Top-level comments: The biggest adjustment for most students is CSCI 142 (Computer Science II). It moves fast, covers object-oriented design, inheritance, interfaces, and data structures all in one semester. If 141 felt easy, do not coast — 142 is where a lot of students hit their first wall. Office hours are essential. CSCI 243 (The Art and Practice of Computer Science) is the course that filters out students who are not serious. You go deep on memory management, pointers, and systems-level thinking in C. It requires CSCI 142 with a C or higher for good reason. Block off serious study time and do not let assignments pile up. CSCI 262 (Introduction to Computer Science Theory) runs alongside 243 for many students and covers formal languages, automata, and computability theory. It is abstract and notation-heavy. Students who struggle tend to be those who have not thought mathematically before — MATH 190 is the prerequisite, and you need to actually absorb it. MATH 190 (Discrete Mathematics for Computing) is not a CS course but it is load-bearing. Sets, logic, proofs by induction, graph theory — all of these come back in 262, in algorithms, and in later systems courses. Take it seriously even if it feels theoretical. CSCI 261 (Analysis of Algorithms) is the course most students say is the most important for technical interviews. Big-O, sorting, graph algorithms, dynamic programming — this is core computer science that every employer tests. Students who struggled here say they underestimated the proof component. For CSCI 250 (Computer Organization), expect to learn digital logic, assembly, memory hierarchies, and how software maps to hardware. It is dense but clarifies a lot about why programs behave the way they do. The lab component takes real time. A common mistake is taking 243, 262, and MATH 251 all in the same semester. Each is individually manageable, but together they leave almost no slack. Many students recommend staggering them or pairing one of them with

learn digital logic, assembly, memory hierarchies, and how software maps to hardware. It is dense but clarifies a lot about why programs behave the way they do. The lab component takes real time. A common mistake is taking 243, 262, and MATH 251 all in the same semester. Each is individually manageable, but together they leave almost no slack. Many students recommend staggering them or pairing one of them with lighter electives. The co-op seminar (CSCI 99) is required and runs every semester. It is low credit but do not ignore the career development deadlines — resume reviews and mock interviews count and employers actually come to campus events. Professors vary significantly in their teaching style for core courses. Rate My Professors is useful for getting a general sense of grading strictness, but take extreme reviews (very positive or very negative) with a grain of salt. The syllabus and sample exams tell you more about workload than any review. For students who want to accelerate: placing out of 141 is possible with a strong AP CS score, and some students attempt 142 and MATH 181 simultaneously in their first semester. Most advise against overloading in year one — the transition to RIT's pace catches people off guard. The CS department has tutoring resources and TAs for most core classes. Use them early, not the night before an exam. Many TAs are upper-level students who took the same course and remember what tripped them up. CSCI 472 (Historical and Current Perspectives in Computer Science) is a writing-intensive requirement for CS majors, typically taken in the fourth or fifth year. It counts as one of three required writing-intensive courses. Do not forget it when planning later semesters.

RIT Computer Science BS — Undergraduate Course Flowchart Source: RIT CS Department, CSUndergradFlowChart.pdf (manually transcribed) This document describes the recommended course sequence for the RIT Computer Science BS degree, organized by year. Prerequisites are shown where relevant. Writing-intensive courses, co-op requirements, and general education requirements are noted throughout. FIRST YEAR First Semester: CSCI 141: Computer Science I (introductory programming; no prerequisite) MATH 181: Calculus I Gen Ed: 1st Year Writing Course (URWT 150 or ISTE 110 satisfies this requirement) Gen Ed: Artistic Perspective Gen Ed: Global Perspective YOPS 10: First-year orientation seminar Second Semester: CSCI 142: Computer Science II (prerequisite: CSCI 141 with C or higher) MATH 182: Calculus II (prerequisite: MATH 181 with C or higher) MATH 190: Discrete Mathematics for Computing (co-requisite: MATH 182) Gen Ed: Ethical Perspective Gen Ed: Social Perspective Wellness Activity RIT 365: RIT and Society (1st Year Requirement) SECOND YEAR Third Semester: CSCI 243: The Art and Practice of Computer Science (prerequisite: CSCI 142 with C or higher; covers C programming, memory management, and software design) CSCI 262: Introduction to Computer Science Theory (prerequisite: CSCI 141 and MATH 190; covers formal languages, automata, and computability) MATH 251: Probability and Statistics (prerequisite: MATH 182) Lab Science 1 (Biology, Chemistry, or Physics sequence; see CS handbook for allowed sequences) Gen Ed Elective Co-op Seminar (CSCI 99; required every semester, prepares students for co-op search) Fourth Semester: CSCI 261: Analysis of Algorithms (prerequisite: CSCI 243 and CSCI 262; covers algorithm design, complexity analysis, sorting, graphs, dynamic programming) MATH 241: Linear Algebra for Engineers (prerequisite: MATH 190; also listed as prerequisite for several AI/graphics electives) Lab Science 2 (continuation of the Lab Science sequence) Gen Ed: Natural Science Inquiry Perspective (Lab Science 1 fulfills this) Gen Ed: Mathematical Perspectives (fulfilled by Calculus sequence) Wellness Activity Summer Co-op (CSCI 488; optional but strongly encouraged; semester-length summer work experience) THIRD YEAR Fifth Semester: CSCI 331: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence (prerequisite: CSCI 142 and MATH 190) CSCI 320: Concepts of Parallel and Distributed Systems (prerequisite: CSCI 243 and MATH 190) CSCI 250: Computer Organization (prerequisite: CSCI 243 and MATH 190; covers digital logic, assembly language, memory hierarchy) Open Elective Gen Ed: Immersion (Immersion is a 3-course thematic sequence chosen from approved Gen Ed areas) Semester Co-op (CSCI 499; full-semester on-site or remote work experience) Sixth Semester: Concurrent and Distributed Systems elective or CS Cluster Elective Language and Program Design (CSCI 344

and Distributed Systems (prerequisite: CSCI 243 and MATH 190) CSCI 250: Computer Organization (prerequisite: CSCI 243 and MATH 190; covers digital logic, assembly language, memory hierarchy) Open Elective Gen Ed: Immersion (Immersion is a 3-course thematic sequence chosen from approved Gen Ed areas) Semester Co-op (CSCI 499; full-semester on-site or remote work experience) Sixth Semester: Concurrent and Distributed Systems elective or CS Cluster Elective Language and Program Design (CSCI 344 or equivalent; prerequisite: CSCI 243 and MATH 243) Open Elective Gen Ed: Immersion continuation Semester Co-op (CSCI 499; another rotation if on co-op cycle) FOURTH AND FIFTH YEAR Seventh and Eighth (or Ninth) Semester: CS Cluster Elective (choose from AIN, AOS, CGM, DSC, DSS, PLT, SEC, or THR clusters; at least two cluster electives required) CS Elective (any upper-division CSCI course not used for cluster) CS Cluster Elective (second cluster course) Open Elective Open Elective CSCI 472: Historical and Current Perspectives in Computer Science (writing-intensive; required for 4th or 5th year standing; satisfies one of three required writing-intensive courses) Gen Ed Elective Gen Ed: Immersion (completion of 3-course Immersion sequence) Semester Co-op (CSCI 499; additional rotations as scheduled) COURSE COLOR/TYPE LEGEND Computer Science / Software Engineering courses: core CS curriculum (CSCI prefix) Math courses: MATH sequence supporting CS foundations General Education: perspective, immersion, and writing requirements Science: Lab Science sequences (Biology, Chemistry, or Physics) Open Elective: any credit-bearing course at RIT First Year Requirement: YOPS 10 and RIT 365 Wellness: physical education or wellness activity Co-op: CSCI 99 (seminar, every semester), CSCI 488 (summer co-op), CSCI 499 (semester co-op) KEY NOTES Writing-Intensive Courses: Three are required. The first-year writing course (URWT 150 or ISTE 110) counts as one. One Gen Ed course must be writing-intensive. CSCI 472 (Historical and Current Perspectives in Computer Science) is the program-specific writing-intensive course and must be completed. Lab Sciences: Only specific sequences are allowed. Biology, Chemistry, and Physics sequences each have approved two-course progressions. See the CS Undergraduate Handbook for the list of allowed sequences. Perspectives: Lab Science 1 fulfills the required Natural Science Inquiry Perspective. The Calculus sequence (MATH 181 and MATH 182) fulfills the Mathematical Perspectives requirement. Prerequisites: Prerequisites are shown in parentheses under course names on the official flowchart. Students must satisfy prerequisites before enrolling. The CS department enforces prerequisite requirements and does not routinely grant exceptions. Co-op Eligibility: Students must maintain satisfactory academic progress and complete co-op seminar (CSCI 99)

sequences. Perspectives: Lab Science 1 fulfills the required Natural Science Inquiry Perspective. The Calculus sequence (MATH 181 and MATH 182) fulfills the Mathematical Perspectives requirement. Prerequisites: Prerequisites are shown in parentheses under course names on the official flowchart. Students must satisfy prerequisites before enrolling. The CS department enforces prerequisite requirements and does not routinely grant exceptions. Co-op Eligibility: Students must maintain satisfactory academic progress and complete co-op seminar (CSCI 99) requirements each semester to remain eligible for co-op rotations. Missing co-op seminar deadlines can delay co-op placement.

Title: CS minor — which electives to take? Subreddit: rit Source: RIT CS student discussion (curated summary) Post body: I'm a non-CS major considering a CS minor. Does anyone know which electives actually satisfy the minor requirements and which ones are worth taking? I have room for about two free electives and I want to make them count. Top-level comments: The CS minor at RIT requires completing a set of core CS courses (including CSCI 141 and 142 as the foundation) plus a specified number of CS elective credits. The exact credit count and elective list is in the CS Undergraduate Handbook — read it carefully because not every CSCI-numbered course automatically counts. For electives within the minor, you want courses that either build depth in one area or give you skills that employers recognize. CSCI 335 (Machine Learning), CSCI 351 (Data Communications and Networks), and CSCI 352 (Operating Systems) are popular choices because they appear on job descriptions constantly. CSCI 455 (Principles of Cybersecurity) is worth considering if you have any interest in security or just want a broad, marketable skill. It covers threat modeling, cryptographic basics, and common attack vectors. Many students from other majors find it accessible and interesting. If you are in a STEM field, CSCI 420 (Principles of Data Mining) pairs well with statistics or engineering backgrounds. It covers clustering, classification, and regression in a CS context. The prerequisite is CSCI 320, so plan accordingly. CSCI 510 (Introduction to Computer Graphics) is popular among design or game-related majors. It is math-heavy (linear algebra shows up constantly), but students who complete it have something concrete and visual to show in portfolios. CSCI 462 (Introduction to Cryptography) is a theory-forward course that satisfies the Security cluster. If you like math and want to understand how encryption actually works at a technical level, this is well regarded. A common mistake is picking electives based purely on interest without checking prerequisites. Courses like CSCI 420, 452, and 453 all require 243 or similar. If you have not taken those foundational courses, your choices narrow significantly. Students who want to maximize career impact with a minor typically pick two or three electives from a single cluster rather than spreading across clusters. Depth signals specialization to employers more than breadth does. The AIN (Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning) cluster is the most popular right now because of the job market. CSCI

require 243 or similar. If you have not taken those foundational courses, your choices narrow significantly. Students who want to maximize career impact with a minor typically pick two or three electives from a single cluster rather than spreading across clusters. Depth signals specialization to employers more than breadth does. The AIN (Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning) cluster is the most popular right now because of the job market. CSCI 335, 534, and 539 seminar offerings that cover large language models are all in demand. Talk to your academic advisor before finalizing choices. The minor requirements can interact with your major's free elective policy, and some courses count for both, which affects how many additional credits you actually need. Upper-level CS courses (500-level) are advanced and can include graduate students. They move faster and assume more background. Students who jump to 500-level electives without the 300/400-level foundation often struggle. Taking CSCI 261 (Analysis of Algorithms) as part of the minor is worth it even though it is tough — it is the most universally tested topic in technical interviews across all CS-adjacent career paths.

Title: Second year CS course flow — is it too much? Subreddit: rit Source: RIT CS student discussion (curated summary) Post body: I'm planning my second year schedule and the default flowchart has me taking CSCI 243, CSCI 262, and MATH 251 all in the same semester on top of gen eds. That seems like a lot. Is this actually manageable or should I spread it out? Top-level comments: That combination — 243, 262, and 251 simultaneously — is genuinely demanding. It is what the flowchart recommends, and some students handle it fine, but there is a meaningful number who end up dropping one mid-semester. Assess honestly how your first year went before committing to all three. CSCI 243 (The Art and Practice of Computer Science) is probably the most time-consuming of the three. Projects in C take longer than you expect, especially if you have not done manual memory management before. Budget ten to fifteen hours per week minimum when projects are due. CSCI 262 (Theory) has a steeper abstract math curve than most students expect. It is not about coding at all — it is proofs, formal grammars, Turing machines, and complexity classes. Students who read ahead and do practice problems do noticeably better than those who only attend lecture. MATH 251 (Probability and Statistics for CS) is the lightest of the three in terms of raw time, but the material is genuinely new for most students. The stats concepts come back in machine learning and data science courses, so do not just memorize formulas — understand the distributions. If you struggled in CSCI 142 or got below a B, seriously consider taking 243 first and shifting 262 to the following semester. The C-or-higher prerequisite on 243 exists for a reason. The co-op seminar (CSCI 99) typically runs alongside your coursework and requires you to attend career workshops, complete resume drafts, and participate in mock interviews. It does not cost much time per week, but the deadlines are fixed — missing them affects your co-op eligibility. Many students take a Lab Science course in their second year to satisfy the science requirement. Lab Science 1 is often Biology, Chemistry, or Physics — all of which have labs that eat additional hours. If you are already stacked with 243/262/251, pairing with a heavy lab section is risky. Students who spread the load more — for example, taking 243 in

fixed — missing them affects your co-op eligibility. Many students take a Lab Science course in their second year to satisfy the science requirement. Lab Science 1 is often Biology, Chemistry, or Physics — all of which have labs that eat additional hours. If you are already stacked with 243/262/251, pairing with a heavy lab section is risky. Students who spread the load more — for example, taking 243 in one semester, then 262 and 251 the following semester — consistently report lower stress and better grades. The tradeoff is potentially delaying co-op eligibility if certain prerequisites are not met. The second-year summer co-op (CSCI 488) is a semester-length work experience. To be eligible, you need to have completed enough coursework and the co-op seminar requirements. If you fall behind on prerequisites, you push your co-op cycle back, which cascades through the rest of your schedule. Joining a student organization or research lab in the second year is good for resume-building, but be realistic about bandwidth. Second year is when the academic pressure peaks for most CS students. Office hours and TAs are underutilized in second year. Professors for 243 and 262 in particular tend to offer meaningful help during office hours — not just hints, but actual concept clarification that changes how you approach the material. Talking to upperclassmen about their experience in specific sections of 243 and 262 is more useful than Rate My Professors for second-year courses. Ask what the exams look like, how project grading is structured, and whether late policies are flexible.

Title: Computer Science BS co-ops — how do they work and how do I prepare? Subreddit: rit Source: RIT CS student discussion (curated summary) Post body: I'm a first-year CS student trying to understand the co-op requirement. What exactly is required, when does it happen, what kinds of companies hire RIT CS students, and what should I be doing now to prepare? Top-level comments: RIT's CS program requires co-op experience as part of the degree. The standard structure involves at least one semester-length co-op (CSCI 499) and optionally a summer co-op (CSCI 488). The co-op seminar (CSCI 99) runs each semester to prepare you — it is a zero-credit requirement but real deadlines apply. Most CS students do their first co-op after sophomore year. To be eligible, you need to have completed enough core coursework and satisfied the co-op seminar requirements, including a completed resume and at least one mock interview session through the university career center. The range of companies that hire RIT CS co-ops is wide: large tech companies (Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, Apple all recruit at RIT), mid-size software companies, defense contractors, financial firms, and local Rochester-area companies. RIT's co-op fair in fall and spring is a major pipeline — attend both. Internships and co-ops are not the same at RIT. A co-op is a full semester off campus working full-time; it counts toward graduation requirements and appears on your transcript. Summer positions (CSCI 488) are shorter and optional but still count. To prepare for co-op recruiting as a first-year: start building a GitHub portfolio now. Even small personal projects — a CLI tool, a web scraper, a data visualization — are worth more than a blank profile. Employers look for evidence that you code outside of class. Technical interviews for CS co-ops typically test data structures and algorithms at the level of CSCI 261 content: arrays, linked lists, trees, graphs, sorting, and dynamic programming. LeetCode Easy to Medium difficulty is the right preparation range for first co-ops. Start practicing in sophomore year, not the week before interviews. Soft skills matter more than most CS students expect. Communication, ability to ask good questions, and professionalism in emails and video calls all affect whether you get an offer and whether a co-op converts to a return offer. The career center's mock interview practice is worth doing seriously. Students who struggle to land co-ops often have one of three

co-ops. Start practicing in sophomore year, not the week before interviews. Soft skills matter more than most CS students expect. Communication, ability to ask good questions, and professionalism in emails and video calls all affect whether you get an offer and whether a co-op converts to a return offer. The career center's mock interview practice is worth doing seriously. Students who struggle to land co-ops often have one of three problems: no visible projects, weak resume formatting, or poor interview performance on basic algorithm questions. All three are fixable with effort. Go to the career center for resume feedback — it is free and the advisors are experienced with CS recruiting. Co-op salaries for RIT CS students are competitive. Expect $20–$40 per hour for first co-ops depending on location, company size, and role. Remote roles at larger companies tend to pay at the high end. Many students use co-op earnings to reduce loan burden. After a successful co-op, document what you built and what you learned in specific, measurable terms before you leave: lines of code contributed, features shipped, percentage improvement in performance, whatever applies. Vague descriptions hurt you in future interviews. RIT has a strong alumni network in tech, especially in the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic. Reaching out to RIT alumni at companies you are interested in is a legitimate and often effective strategy — many are happy to refer students for open co-op roles. Some students do all their required co-ops remotely and never leave campus for an extended period. Remote co-ops can be fine but you miss out on on-site networking. If you get a chance to do an in-person rotation, consider taking it.

Title: Best CS cluster electives — what are students actually recommending? Subreddit: rit Source: RIT CS student discussion (curated summary) Post body: I need to choose a CS cluster and pick electives. There are eight clusters (AIN, AOS, CGM, DSC, DSS, PLT, SEC, THR) and I do not know enough to make an informed choice. What are students saying about each one, and which electives within them are actually worth taking? Top-level comments: The AIN (Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning) cluster is the most popular right now by a large margin. CSCI 335 (Machine Learning), CSCI 420 (Principles of Data Mining), CSCI 431 (Computer Vision), and CSCI 534 (Mobile Robot Programming) are the heavy hitters. If you want to work in ML, data science, or any AI-adjacent role, this cluster maps directly to what employers are hiring for. The coursework is math-heavy — expect linear algebra and probability to matter. The SEC (Security) cluster is consistently well-regarded for both career relevance and intellectual interest. CSCI 455 (Principles of Cybersecurity), CSCI 462 (Introduction to Cryptography), CSCI 532 (Intelligent Security Systems), and CSCI 642 (Secure Coding) are the core offerings. Security roles are in demand and the pay is strong. Students who combine SEC with systems courses are particularly attractive to employers. The DSC (Data Science) cluster overlaps significantly with AIN but is more focused on data infrastructure, cleaning, and statistical analysis. CSCI 420, CSCI 521 (Data Cleaning and Preparation), and CSCI 536 (Information Retrieval) are the key courses. Good choice if your career interest is in data engineering or analytics rather than pure ML research. The AOS (Architecture and Operating Systems) cluster covers the deepest systems content in the CS curriculum: CSCI 452 (Systems Programming), CSCI 453 (Computer Architecture), CSCI 352 (Operating Systems), and CSCI 654 (Parallel Computing). Students who complete this cluster deeply understand how software runs on hardware. It is considered one of the harder clusters but highly respected by systems-focused employers. The PLT (Programming Languages and Tools) cluster attracts students who like compilers, language design, and advanced programming paradigms. CSCI 464 (XTreme Theory), CSCI 541 Advanced Programming skill courses (Go, C++, Rust, Haskell, C#), and related seminars are the options. The 541 series is unique — each offering covers a different language/paradigm and can be taken multiple times. Students who want to be strong language generalists or work in compiler/toolchain engineering gravitate here. The CGM (Computer Graphics and

attracts students who like compilers, language design, and advanced programming paradigms. CSCI 464 (XTreme Theory), CSCI 541 Advanced Programming skill courses (Go, C++, Rust, Haskell, C#), and related seminars are the options. The 541 series is unique — each offering covers a different language/paradigm and can be taken multiple times. Students who want to be strong language generalists or work in compiler/toolchain engineering gravitate here. The CGM (Computer Graphics and Computational Media) cluster is best for students interested in game development, simulation, or visual computing. CSCI 510 (Computer Graphics), CSCI 511 (Interactive Music Experiences), and CSCI 631 (Computer Vision foundations) are popular. It requires a solid math foundation — linear algebra and some calculus are prerequisites in effect even if not always listed formally. The DSS (Distributed Systems) cluster focuses on networked and concurrent computing: CSCI 351 (Data Communications and Networks), CSCI 652 (Distributed Systems), and CSCI 654 (Parallel Computing). Cloud infrastructure, microservices, and large-scale system design are all grounded in this material. Strong choice for students aiming at backend engineering or cloud roles. The THR (Theory) cluster is the most abstract option. It covers computability, complexity, and mathematical foundations of CS. CSCI 262 is often the gateway course, and upper-level Theory options include CSCI 569 seminar offerings and CSCI 464. Students who love math and are considering graduate school often gravitate here. A common pattern among students who are happy with their cluster choice: they picked based on what they would actually enjoy learning, not just what sounds impressive. Clusters are studied over two or more semesters — choosing one you are not interested in leads to grinding through courses you hate. You do not have to commit to a cluster in your first or second year. Most cluster electives are 400-level or above, so you have time to explore during your first two years and then decide based on what clicked. Some students take a survey of two or three areas before committing. Talk to upperclassmen who have completed a cluster before finalizing your choice. They can tell you which professors make the material click, which courses have the heaviest workload, and whether the content actually showed up in their interviews or internships.
